Name: jacobian_guided_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping for Latency-Aware Inference
Short Hypothesis: Jacobian-based sensitivity analysis can dynamically identify and skip redundant layers in deep neural networks during inference, significantly reducing latency without compromising accuracy.
Related Work: Existing methods for layer skipping often rely on static heuristics or require additional training, which limits their adaptability and efficiency. Training-free approaches like token pruning and caching have shown promise but lack a systematic way to dynamically assess layer importance. Our proposal leverages Jacobian-based sensitivity analysis to dynamically determine layer redundancy, distinguishing it from prior work by providing a principled, training-free, and latency-aware solution.
Abstract: Deep neural networks, particularly transformer-based models, suffer from high inference latency due to their sequential layer processing. While layer skipping offers a potential solution, existing methods often rely on static heuristics or require additional training, limiting their adaptability. We propose a novel training-free framework that leverages Jacobian-based sensitivity analysis to dynamically identify and skip redundant layers during inference. By computing the Jacobian matrix of each layer's output with respect to its input, we quantify the layer's contribution to the final prediction and dynamically skip layers that minimally affect the output. This approach allows for significant latency reduction without compromising accuracy. We validate our method on vision transformers (ViTs) and large language models (LLMs), demonstrating up to 2x speedup with less than 1% accuracy loss. Our framework provides a principled, latency-aware solution for efficient inference, bridging the gap between theory and practical deployment.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the framework on ViTs (e.g., ViT-B/16) using ImageNet-1K. Compute Jacobian matrices for each layer and dynamically skip layers based on sensitivity thresholds. Measure accuracy drop and latency reduction.
Experiment 2: Apply the framework to LLMs (e.g., GPT-3) on text generation tasks. Assess the impact of layer skipping on perplexity and inference speed. Compare with static layer skipping baselines.
Experiment 3: Ablation study to analyze the impact of different sensitivity thresholds on accuracy and latency. Identify the optimal trade-off between speed and performance.
Experiment 4: Compare the proposed method with existing training-free acceleration techniques (e.g., token pruning, caching) in terms of accuracy, latency, and memory usage.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead, potentially offsetting latency gains. Mitigation: Use efficient approximations or precompute Jacobians for fixed inputs.
Risk 2: Dynamic layer skipping may lead to instability in certain models or tasks. Mitigation: Implement fallback mechanisms to ensure minimal accuracy loss.
Limitation 1: The method may be less effective for models with highly interdependent layers, where skipping one layer affects subsequent layers significantly.
Limitation 2: The framework requires access to layer-wise outputs, which may not be feasible for all architectures or deployment scenarios.
